items.title: Test that a valid token is accepted and an invalid token is rejected
items.description: Write tests that assert the authorisation gate: a connection presenting the correct session token reaches streaming state; a connection with a wrong, empty, or expired token is rejected before any frame is sent. This locks the core security invariant in place.
items.applicability: Automated tests for the server's token-validation path.
items.example: TokenValidator_Accepts_CorrectToken and TokenValidator_Rejects_WrongToken assert true/false; an integration test confirms a bad-token socket is closed with zero frames received.
items.limitation: Unit tests confirm the gate logic but not that every code path routes through it; a source/route review is still needed to ensure no endpoint bypasses validation.

items.title: Test frame serialization round-trips losslessly
items.description: Serialize a ScreenFrame (metadata + JPEG bytes) and deserialize it, asserting the session id, frame number, dimensions, timestamp, and payload bytes match. This guards the wire format against accidental breakage.
items.applicability: Unit tests of the frame codec used by the protocol.
items.example: A test builds a frame with FrameNumber=42 and a known byte payload, serializes then deserializes it, and asserts every field including the byte array is equal.
items.limitation: A round-trip test validates the serializer against itself; it cannot catch a divergence from a different language/version reader unless that reader is also tested against the same fixtures.

items.title: Test in-process loopback streaming with the fake frame source
items.description: Stand up the server and a client in-process, connect over a loopback WebSocket, drive frames from the deterministic fake source, and assert that frames arrive and the frame counter increments across the burst. This proves the end-to-end pipeline without a real desktop.
items.applicability: Integration test of capture-to-viewer streaming in CI.
items.example: The test starts the server on 127.0.0.1, connects a client using FakeScreenSource, reads 10 frames, and asserts the received FrameNumber sequence strictly increases.
items.limitation: Loopback removes real network conditions (loss, latency, NAT); it validates protocol correctness but not field behaviour over an actual internet path.

items.title: Test that Disconnect stops the stream
items.description: Assert that after the client sends Disconnect (or the user triggers it), no further frames are produced or sent and the session is torn down. This verifies the user's stop control actually stops sharing.
items.applicability: Integration test of the teardown path.
items.example: The test streams a few frames, calls Disconnect, waits, and asserts the received frame count stops increasing and the server marks the session closed.
items.limitation: The test sees frames already in flight at disconnect time; it must assert 'no NEW frames after a short grace window' rather than an exact instantaneous cutoff.

items.title: Test the dashboard health endpoint returns 200
items.description: A smoke test requests the server/dashboard health endpoint and asserts an HTTP 200 with the expected status shape. This catches gross startup/wiring failures fast and is the cheapest liveness signal.
items.applicability: CI smoke test of the operator dashboard/server health route.
items.example: GET /health returns 200 with { status:'ok', sessions:0 }; the test fails the build if the status code is not 200.
items.limitation: A 200 health response proves the process is up and routing, not that streaming actually works; it must be paired with the loopback streaming test for real coverage.

items.title: Run a Playwright smoke test asserting no HTTP 500
items.description: Drive the operator dashboard with Playwright, visit the key pages, and assert none return HTTP 500 and no page shows an error state. This catches server-side exceptions in the UI before a human does.
items.applicability: End-to-end UI smoke testing of the dashboard.
items.example: A Playwright test navigates to the dashboard home and sessions pages, capturing responses, and fails if any response status is >= 500.
items.limitation: A no-500 smoke test confirms pages render without server errors but does not validate correctness of the displayed values; assertions on specific content are needed for that.

items.title: Run a security source-scan test for forbidden APIs
items.description: An automated test greps the PRODUCT source for forbidden capabilities and fails the build if any appear: keyboard hooks (SetWindowsHookEx, WH_KEYBOARD_LL, GetAsyncKeyState), clipboard access (OpenClipboard, GetClipboardData), file-exfiltration patterns, input injection (SendInput, keybd_event, mouse_event), and webcam/microphone capture. This mechanically enforces the consent-security rules.
items.applicability: A CI guard test over the product source tree.
items.example: The test scans *.cs for the forbidden-symbol list and asserts zero matches outside test fixtures; a pull request that adds SetWindowsHookEx fails CI immediately.
items.limitation: A textual scan can be evaded by reflection, P/Invoke string-building, or a compiled dependency; it is a strong tripwire against accidental or obvious additions, not a complete proof of absence, so dependencies must stay minimal and reviewed.

items.title: Scope the source-scan to product code, not tests or fixtures
items.description: The forbidden-API scan must target the shipping product source and exclude test projects and fixtures, so that a test which merely names a forbidden symbol (to assert it is absent) does not cause a false failure. Define the include/exclude paths explicitly.
items.applicability: Configuration of the security source-scan test.
items.example: The scan includes src/ProductClient and src/ProductServer and excludes tests/ so the assertion string 'SetWindowsHookEx' inside the scan test itself is not counted.
items.limitation: Mis-scoped excludes could hide real product code under a 'tests' path; the exclude list must be precise and reviewed so the scan cannot be neutered by relocating code.

items.title: Capture dashboard screenshots in Playwright runs
items.description: Configure Playwright to capture a screenshot of each key dashboard page (and on failure) so a human can quickly confirm the UI looks right and diagnose failures from CI artefacts without re-running locally.
items.applicability: Playwright test configuration for the dashboard.
items.example: On each test the runner saves dashboard-home.png and, on failure, failure-<test>.png to the artefacts folder for review.
items.limitation: Screenshots aid human review but are not assertions; a visually broken layout still passes unless an explicit check (or visual-diff baseline) fails on it.

items.title: Design for a 15-year-old: double-click only, no command line
items.description: The end user who shares their screen must be able to start with a single double-click and never need a terminal, flags, or configuration editing. Anything technical is pre-baked into the generated package. The bar is a capable 15-year-old, not a developer.
items.applicability: Usability requirement for the recipient-facing client experience.
items.example: The user receives a folder, double-clicks 'Start Screen Sharing.exe', clicks Allow on the firewall prompt, and is sharing — with no typing involved.
items.limitation: Some environments still force technical steps (corporate firewall, no rights); where that happens the operator, not the end user, must handle it, and the tool should say so clearly rather than expecting the user to cope.

items.title: Use simple buttons and clear, plain-language messages
items.description: The client UI uses a few large, clearly labelled buttons (Start Sharing, Disconnect) and plain-language status messages ('You are sharing your screen', 'Sharing stopped', 'Could not connect — ask your helper'). No error codes or jargon facing the end user.
items.applicability: Client UI copy and control design for non-technical users.
items.example: Instead of 'WebSocket 1006 abnormal closure', the client shows 'The connection dropped. Click Start Sharing to try again or contact your helper.'
items.limitation: Plain messages must still be accurate; over-simplifying can hide a real cause the operator needs, so technical detail belongs in a log the operator can read, not the end-user message.

items.title: Generate a checklist for any technical step the user must take
items.description: Whenever the user must do something technical (allow the firewall, read a session code, tell the operator their LAN address, or — operator side — set a port-forward), the package generates a tailored, numbered checklist for exactly that step, so nobody has to improvise.
items.applicability: Per-step guidance bundled with the generated client/operator package.
items.example: If the session needs a port-forward, the generator emits 'Port-forward checklist: 1) Open your router admin. 2) Forward external 8080 to 192.168.1.50:8080. 3) Tell your helper your public IP.'
items.limitation: A generated checklist reflects the configuration known at generation time; if the environment differs (different router, changed IP) the steps may not match exactly and the operator must adapt them.

items.title: Provide a troubleshooting checklist for failed connections
items.description: Ship a short, ordered troubleshooting checklist covering the common failure causes in likely order: firewall not allowed, wrong/expired token, server not running, wrong address/port, not on the same network (LAN mode), and no public reachability (internet mode). Each item names the symptom and the fix.
items.example: Troubleshooting.txt: 'Can't connect? 1) Did you click Allow on the firewall? 2) Is the server running? 3) Same Wi-Fi? 4) Correct address and port? 5) Internet: is the port forwarded and TLS on?'
items.limitation: A checklist covers common cases in a typical order but cannot diagnose every environment; persistent failures may need the operator to read server logs, which the checklist should point to as the next step.

items.title: Show clear connection status and next action to the user
items.description: The client always shows the current state — Connecting, Sharing, Not sharing, or a friendly error — and tells the user the single next thing to do. The user should never be left guessing whether sharing is on or what to do next.
items.applicability: State-to-message mapping in the client UI.
items.example: While connecting the client shows 'Connecting to your helper…'; on success 'You are sharing your primary screen — click Disconnect to stop'; on failure a one-line cause and next action.
items.limitation: Status reflects the client's knowledge; a network partition can leave the client briefly showing 'Sharing' after the viewer has actually lost the stream until the heartbeat timeout corrects it.

items.title: Keep an operator log separate from end-user messages
items.description: Detailed technical events (handshake failures, token mismatches, timeouts, frame rates) go to an operator-facing log, while the end user sees only simple messages. Separating the two lets the operator diagnose without confusing the user.
items.applicability: Logging and messaging separation across the two audiences.
items.example: The user sees 'Could not connect'; the operator's log records 'Token mismatch from 192.168.1.77, connection closed before handshake' with a timestamp.
items.limitation: Logs must avoid recording secrets (full tokens, screen contents) and personal data; a log that captures sensitive material becomes its own security and privacy liability.

items.title: Verify the end-to-end happy path before claiming done
items.description: Before declaring a build ready, run the full happy path: generate a client package, start the server, allow the firewall, connect on the LAN, confirm the viewer sees increasing frames, press Disconnect, and confirm the stream stops. Automated tests cover units; this manual pass covers the real experience.
items.applicability: Release acceptance checklist for a screen-share build.
items.example: The acceptance run records: package generated (ok), server started (ok), firewall allowed (ok), LAN frames increasing (ok), Disconnect stops stream (ok) — only then is the build marked done.
items.limitation: A successful LAN happy path does not prove internet reachability or performance under load; those require their own explicit runs and the honest reachability caveats from the network pack.
